onthological_analysis.title: onthological analysis
onthological_analysis.contents:
Block by Block is a public program run jointly by UN-Habitat and Mojang that integrates the video game Minecraft into public space planning in order to draw community members more directly into the design process. Its premise nestles on accessibility: Minecraft's three-dimentional environment allows people with no design training to visualize their surroundings, explore possibilities, discuss together, and tentatively manipulate space directly. As the project's own materials put it, Minecraft helps residents "model their surroundings, visualize possibilities, express ideas, drive consensus, and accelerate progress."

But naming the actors involved already complicates this description. The process moves through UN-Habitat, Mojang, Minecraft as a platform, residents, local governments, other stakeholders, and expert architects and design studios. A typical workshop runs through pre-workshop preparation (site selection, data collection, technical prerequisites(local)), the workshop itself (introduction, site visit(together with local participants and staff), teaching Minecraft, presentation/prioritization), and a post-workshop report. Read this way, the workshop is not simply a design exercise; it is a governance procedure–training, deliberation, presentation, and settlement among residents, stakeholders, executors, each with different degrees of authority over what services.

This means Block by Block is not one thing but several layered ones at once. It is a design method giving untrained hands a medium for expressing spatial ideas. Resonating with Minecraft itself as a procedural geometry, this project is a procedural filter, in that the workshop structure funnels resident input through stages of review and approval before anything becomes buildable. And it is an operation running on commercial platform infrastructure: the entire process takes place inside software owned by Microsoft, generating goodwill, press coverage, and SDG-aligned legitimacy for that platform alongside whatever plazas eventually get built. None of these three identities cancels each other out.

historical_analysis.title: historical/contextual analysis
historical_analysis.contents:
UN-Habitat, the United Nations Human Settlements Programme, exists to promote socially and environmentally sustainable towns and cities and to advocate for adequate shelter for all. Its Global Public Space Programme consolidates an integrated approach to public space improvement–inclusion, connectivity, environmental sustainability, and safety–and ties this to site-specific assessment, using local data to identify performance targets for the future. Block by Block sits inside this larger institutional mission, but its own genealogy is more specific. It descends from a Swedish precedent, Mina Kvarter(My Blocks), which tested Minecraft for youth participation in redesigning low-cost housing. The global program began with pilots in Kenya and Nepal, then scaled significantly after Microsoft's 2015 acquisition of Mojang, at which point a dedicated foundation was formed to expand its reach.

Each workshop mobilizes marginalized groups into a process framed as civic strengthening. But this framing deserves scrutiny. The figure of "220 minecrafters" is offered as evidence of empowerment, yet final designs and decisions are still negotiated and ratified by stakeholders–fovernment officials, professional design studios, funders whether public or private. Their approval, not residents' block component placements in Minecraft, is the actual bottleneck of implementation. Participation in the game does not by itself redistribute the decision-making power that determines where a budget goes.

The involvement of a private company like Microsoft at one end, and of officials and funders at the other, is largely absent from the project's public-facing narrative, which instead foregrounds the community-oriented workshop stage. This is not to say political and institutional relationships are avoidable once actual construction is at stake, yet execution requires authority, budget, negotiation, and some hierarchy is inevitable. But private-sector mediation specifically is not: if UN-Habitat built its own open visualization tool rather than relying on Minecraft, wouldn't its message reach the world more directly, without folding a private company's platform interests into a public process?

At the same time, the project's benefits are real. In places like Kenya, where refugees, children, and women facing structural inequality are often excluded from planning processes, Block by Block has offered an environment for these groups to actively express their presence and take part in shaping decisions. But the same process that elevates these voices also generates data(about residents, preferences, spatial behavior) that flows toward a larger set of institutional and corporate entities. How UN-Habitat or Microsoft uses, stores, or shares this data is not made transparent. The empowerment and the extraction are not separable.

visual_analysis.title: visual/aesthetic analysis
visual_analysis.contents:
Livability, raw.

The designs produced in Block by Block workshops are not sophisticated, and this is by design as much as by necessity, and are completed in rough, basic arrangements of boxes and configurations. Minecraft's voxel style itself is coarse: no rendering sophistication, no professional polish. This crudeness can be read as a sign of democratic access rather than a limitation–an aesthetic that signals that expertise is not the price of entry.

What stands out is that even within this simple vocabulary of color and block form, residents manage to encode a real sense of livability into their designs. Such as "Pop-Up Playgrounds for Kids in Hanoi" or "Building Family-Friendly Walkways in São Paulo" show legibility once applied to an actual site, visually announcing its own adaptation to place. But this legibility comes at a cost that is easy to miss: the very act of reading blocky proposals as "livable" or "community-driven" strips away the fact that they are produced inside, and dependent on, Mojang's commercial engine. Color, texture, spatial nuance, and the affective register of a community's workshop and vision are ultimately compressed into the vocabulary of permits and construction drawings. A private company's software mediates the translation of a public vision into an institutionally legible form.
